clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of worsening sore throat, odynophagia, and fever of 39.2°C. He appears toxic and is drooling saliva. Examination of the oropharynx is unremarkable with no visible tonsil or pharyngeal exudate. His oxygen saturation is 94% on air and he has inspiratory stridor. He is fully vaccinated. What is the single most important immediate management priority?
options: Perform throat swab and start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate
Secure the airway and hospitalise with IV antibiotics and steroids
Arrange lateral soft tissue neck X-ray urgently before any intervention
Administer nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 and discharge with close follow-up
Refer urgently to gastroenterology for oesophagoscopy
explanation: This adult presentation — severe odynophagia, drooling, stridor, and a normal-appearing oropharynx — is classic for acute epiglottitis (supraglottitis). Hospitalisation is essential because of the danger of complete airway obstruction. Airway must be secured first (intubation or tracheostomy if needed), followed by parenteral antibiotics (ampicillin or third-generation cephalosporin) and IV steroids to reduce oedema. Diagnosis is confirmed with fibre-optic nasendoscopy in the awake adult.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics are inappropriate in a patient with impending airway obstruction and systemic toxicity; parenteral antibiotics and airway control take priority.
This is correct — securing the airway and hospitalising the patient with IV antibiotics and steroids is the immediate management priority in acute epiglottitis.
A lateral neck X-ray can confirm the thumbprint sign but must never delay airway management; sending an unstable patient to radiology risks fatal obstruction.
Nebulised adrenaline is the treatment for croup (subglottic), not for supraglottic epiglottitis; discharging this patient would be extremely dangerous.
Oesophagoscopy is irrelevant and would dangerously delay definitive airway management.
clinicalPearl: In adults, severe sore throat with drooling and a normal oropharynx on inspection should immediately raise suspicion for acute epiglottitis — secure the airway before any other investigation.
keyClue: Drooling, stridor, normal oropharynx, and severe odynophagia in an adult
commonExamTrap: Confusing this with tonsillitis and prescribing oral antibiotics without securing the airway
missedConcept: The normal oropharyngeal examination in epiglottitis — the pathology is supraglottic and not visible without laryngoscopy
redFlagReason: Acute epiglottitis can cause complete airway obstruction and death within hours; airway is the absolute priority.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old boy presents in November with a 2-day history of barking cough, hoarse voice, and low-grade fever. He has mild inspiratory stridor at rest and mild intercostal recession. His oxygen saturation is 97% on air. He is alert and not drooling. An anteroposterior soft tissue neck X-ray shows symmetrical subglottic narrowing. What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological treatment?
options: Intramuscular benzylpenicillin and IV dexamethasone
Nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL and systemic corticosteroids
Oral amoxicillin and saline nebulisation only
Immediate tracheal intubation with a tube one size smaller than expected
IV ceftriaxone and bronchoscopy to exclude foreign body
explanation: The clinical picture — barking cough, hoarse voice, low-grade fever, and steeple sign on X-ray — is diagnostic of croup (laryngo-tracheobronchitis), most commonly caused by parainfluenza viruses. The standard treatment is air humidification, nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 (5 mL) for rapid mucosal vasoconstriction and oedema reduction, plus systemic corticosteroids (oral, IM, or inhaled) which have proven efficacy. Patients given nebulised adrenaline must be reassessed 3–4 hours later for rebound stridor.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Benzylpenicillin is used for diphtheria; this presentation lacks the grey membrane and systemic toxicity of diphtheria. Antibiotics are not indicated for viral croup.
This is correct — nebulised adrenaline and systemic corticosteroids are the evidence-based treatment for moderate croup.
Oral amoxicillin has no role in viral croup; saline nebulisation alone is insufficient for a child with stridor at rest.
Intubation is reserved for severe cases with impending respiratory failure; this child has mild-to-moderate croup with good saturations.
Ceftriaxone and bronchoscopy are not indicated; the clinical and radiological picture is consistent with croup and there is no choking history to suggest foreign body.
clinicalPearl: The steeple sign on AP neck X-ray, barking cough, and low-grade fever in a child aged 6 months to 3 years is croup; treat with nebulised adrenaline plus corticosteroids.
keyClue: Barking cough, steeple sign, and age 2 years in November (viral season)
commonExamTrap: Ordering the neck X-ray before stabilising the airway, or using antibiotics for what is a viral infection
missedConcept: The steeple sign is due to subglottic narrowing — the cricoid is the only complete cartilaginous ring and prevents outward expansion, making croup potentially dangerous

clinicalStem: A 42-year-old woman wakes up one morning to find she cannot hear from her right ear. She reports tinnitus and a brief episode of dizziness. Examination is unremarkable; the tympanic membrane is normal bilaterally. Audiometry shows a 45 dB sensorineural hearing loss over three contiguous frequencies in the right ear. Weber lateralises to the left. No clear cause is identified after initial investigations. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Myringotomy and grommet insertion to the right ear
Oral prednisolone 40–60 mg in a single morning dose for one week, then tapered over three weeks
Low molecular weight dextran infusion immediately
Reassure and review in 6 months as spontaneous recovery is universal
Urgent stapedectomy
explanation: This is sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL), defined as 30 dB or more of SNHL over at least three contiguous frequencies within 72 hours. Where no cause is found (idiopathic), empirical treatment includes steroid therapy — prednisolone 40–60 mg as a single morning dose for one week, then tapered over three weeks. Steroids are anti-inflammatory and reduce cochlear oedema. About 50–60% of patients with idiopathic SSNHL recover spontaneously within 15 days, but early treatment improves prognosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Myringotomy addresses conductive hearing loss from middle ear effusion; the audiogram here shows sensorineural loss with no air-bone gap, making this inappropriate.
This is correct — oral prednisolone 40–60 mg daily for one week, tapered over three weeks, is the first-line empirical treatment for idiopathic sudden SNHL.
Low molecular weight dextran decreases blood viscosity and is one adjunct, but it is contraindicated in cardiac failure and bleeding disorders; it is not the first-line monotherapy.
Spontaneous recovery occurs in 50–60% — not all patients — and chances of recovery are poor after one month; waiting 6 months is inappropriate.
Stapedectomy is used for otosclerosis causing conductive hearing loss, not for sensorineural sudden hearing loss.
clinicalPearl: Idiopathic sudden SNHL should be treated empirically with oral high-dose steroids early; chances of recovery are poor after one month, so do not delay treatment.
keyClue: Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss of sudden onset with no air-bone gap and no identifiable cause
commonExamTrap: Treating with myringotomy or grommet for what appears to be hearing loss without recognising it is sensorineural rather than conductive
missedConcept: Sudden SNHL is a medical emergency requiring early steroid therapy; the presence of vertigo is a poor prognostic sign

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old man with known Meniere's disease presents to the emergency department with a severe attack of rotatory vertigo that started 2 hours ago. He is vomiting profusely and unable to tolerate oral fluids. He describes severe aural fullness and tinnitus in the left ear. On examination there is horizontal nystagmus. His blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg and he is distressed. What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological management?
options: Oral betahistine 16 mg three times daily
Intramuscular prochlorperazine (Stemetil) and IV diazepam if needed
Oral furosemide 40 mg with potassium supplementation
Carbogen inhalation (5% CO2 with 95% O2) only
Intratympanic steroid injection in the left ear
explanation: During an acute attack of Meniere's disease, the patient has severe vertigo with vomiting that precludes oral medication. Vestibular sedatives are the mainstay of acute treatment and must be given parenterally (IM or IV) when vomiting prevents oral administration. Prochlorperazine (Stemetil), promethazine, or dimenhydrinate are appropriate choices; IV diazepam 5–10 mg may also be given. The patient also needs bed rest with head supported to prevent excessive movement.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral betahistine (Vertin) is used in the chronic management phase to increase labyrinthine blood flow; it has no role in the acute attack, especially when the patient is vomiting and cannot take oral medication.
This is correct — parenteral vestibular sedatives (IM prochlorperazine) and IV diazepam if needed are the appropriate acute treatment when vomiting prevents oral administration.
Furosemide is used for chronic Meniere's management to reduce endolymph volume, not during the acute attack.
Carbogen inhalation improves labyrinthine circulation and is an adjunct, but it is insufficient as monotherapy for an acutely vomiting patient with severe vertigo.
Intratympanic steroids are used to raise local cochlear steroid concentration and are a chronic or recurrent phase intervention, not an acute emergency treatment.
clinicalPearl: In an acute Meniere's attack with vomiting, vestibular sedatives must be given parenterally (IM prochlorperazine or IV diazepam) since oral medication cannot be absorbed.
keyClue: Known Meniere's, profuse vomiting precludes oral medication, acute rotatory vertigo
commonExamTrap: Prescribing oral betahistine in the acute attack — betahistine is a chronic phase drug and useless when the patient is vomiting
missedConcept: The route of administration of vestibular sedatives must match the patient's ability to tolerate oral medications; IV or IM routes are essential when vomiting is present

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old male ex-smoker with a 40 pack-year history presents with progressive hoarseness for 8 weeks. He denies dysphagia or weight loss. Examination of the neck reveals no palpable lymphadenopathy. Mirror laryngoscopy shows a white lesion on the free margin of the left true vocal cord with impaired but not absent cord mobility. What is the single most important next step in management?
options: CT scan of the neck and chest to stage the disease before biopsy
Biopsy of the laryngeal lesion under general anaesthesia for histological diagnosis
Trial of voice therapy and reassessment in 12 weeks
PET scan to exclude distant metastasis
Urgent tracheostomy to protect the airway
explanation: Hoarseness lasting more than two weeks in a heavy smoker requires indirect laryngoscopy and biopsy. Biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis of laryngeal cancer, and histological confirmation is essential before any treatment decision. The lesion on the free margin of the true vocal cord with impaired mobility is suspicious for glottic carcinoma. Staging imaging (CT/MRI) follows histological confirmation, not precedes it, though both may be arranged in close sequence.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT staging is important but should follow — not replace — histological diagnosis; biopsy must come first to confirm malignancy before committing to a staging workup.
This is correct — biopsy with histological examination is the gold standard for diagnosis of laryngeal cancer; adequate sampling is critical to avoid sampling error.
A 65-year-old male smoker with 8 weeks of hoarseness and a vocal cord lesion requires urgent biopsy, not watchful waiting — this delay risks progression of potentially curable early-stage cancer.
PET scan is part of the staging workup after histological confirmation and is most useful for detecting occult regional or distant metastasis in known malignancy.
There is no indication for emergency tracheostomy; the airway is not immediately compromised as the patient can phonate and breathe.
clinicalPearl: Hoarseness for more than two weeks in a smoker mandates laryngoscopy; biopsy is the gold standard — histology before staging imaging.
keyClue: White lesion on vocal cord free margin with impaired mobility in a heavy smoker over age 60
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT scan first as the definitive investigation — imaging stages cancer but does not diagnose it; biopsy provides histology
missedConcept: Impaired vocal cord mobility (T2) versus fixed cord (T3) are critical staging distinctions that affect surgical planning in glottic carcinoma

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman presents with brief episodes of severe rotatory vertigo lasting less than one minute, triggered every time she rolls over in bed to the right or looks upward. She denies tinnitus, hearing loss, or any neurological symptoms. The Dix-Hallpike manoeuvre to the right reproduces her symptoms with a brief latency and characteristic nystagmus that fatigues on repetition. What is the most effective treatment for this condition?
options: Long-term oral prochlorperazine (Stemetil) 10 mg three times daily
Betahistine 16 mg three times daily for three months
Epley's manoeuvre to reposition otoconial debris from the posterior semicircular canal into the utricle
Myringotomy and exploratory tympanotomy to assess for perilymph fistula
MRI brain to exclude acoustic neuroma before treatment
explanation: The presentation is classic benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV): brief positional vertigo (under 1 minute), triggered by specific head positions, no hearing loss, and a fatigable positive Dix-Hallpike test. BPPV is caused by calcium carbonate crystals (otoconia) in the posterior semicircular canal. The Epley manoeuvre repositions this debris back into the utricle through a five-step head rotation sequence. A single Epley manoeuvre cures approximately 80% of patients, and the patient should maintain an upright posture for 48 hours afterwards.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Long-term vestibular sedatives are inappropriate for BPPV; they do not address the underlying mechanical cause and may impair vestibular compensation.
Betahistine increases labyrinthine blood flow and is used in Meniere's disease, not BPPV which is a purely mechanical disorder.
This is correct — the Epley manoeuvre repositions otoconial debris from the posterior semicircular canal to the utricle and cures approximately 80% of patients with a single treatment.
Tympanotomy is for perilymph fistula; BPPV features no hearing loss and a characteristic fatigable Hallpike response inconsistent with fistula.
MRI is not required before treating classic BPPV; the typical history and fatigable positive Hallpike test are sufficient for diagnosis and the Epley manoeuvre can proceed immediately.
clinicalPearl: Classic BPPV (fatigable positional nystagmus, no hearing loss) is diagnosed clinically and treated with the Epley manoeuvre — 80% cured with a single treatment.
keyClue: Brief positional vertigo triggered by specific head position, fatiguing on repetition, no hearing loss or tinnitus
commonExamTrap: Prescribing vestibular sedatives long-term rather than performing the mechanical Epley manoeuvre which addresses the root cause
missedConcept: BPPV is a mechanical disorder caused by displaced otoconia, not an endolymphatic or vascular problem — pharmacotherapy does not cure it

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old unvaccinated child presents from a rural community with a 3-day history of low-grade fever, sore throat, and hoarse voice. He is now in marked respiratory distress with inspiratory stridor. Examination shows a greyish-white adherent membrane on the tonsils and soft palate; its attempted removal causes bleeding. He has tachycardia (HR 140 bpm) and a thready pulse. His neck appears swollen. What is the most important immediate treatment?
options: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg immediately and reassess in 4 hours
Diphtheria antitoxin IV as saline infusion after test dose, with benzylpenicillin IM and airway support
Urgent adenotonsillectomy to remove the obstructing membrane
Nebulised adrenaline and oral dexamethasone; discharge with antibiotics
Intubation only; defer antitoxin until culture confirms Corynebacterium diphtheriae
explanation: This is laryngeal diphtheria caused by Corynebacterium diphtheriae. The greyish-white adherent membrane that bleeds on removal, bull-neck appearance (cervical lymphadenopathy), and systemic toxicity (tachycardia, thready pulse) are classic. Immediate treatment requires: (1) diphtheria antitoxin IV (20,000–100,000 units depending on severity) after a test dose — it neutralises circulating free toxin; (2) benzylpenicillin IM for 6 days to kill bacteria; and (3) airway maintenance — tracheostomy may be essential. Treatment must not await culture results. Bed rest for 2–4 weeks is essential to protect against myocarditis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ceftriaxone is not the first-line antibiotic for diphtheria; benzylpenicillin is the treatment of choice. More critically, antitoxin — which neutralises the exotoxin — must be given simultaneously and takes priority.
This is correct — diphtheria antitoxin after test dose (neutralises free toxin), plus benzylpenicillin IM, plus airway support (tracheostomy if needed) constitute the definitive emergency management.
Surgical removal of the membrane is not the treatment; the danger is the exotoxin causing myocarditis and paralysis, not just the membrane itself. Adenotonsillectomy is not indicated.
Nebulised adrenaline is for croup; this patient has diphtheria with systemic toxicity and needs antitoxin urgently. Discharging him would be fatal.
Antitoxin must never be delayed pending culture results; diphtheria diagnosis is clinical and delay increases mortality from toxin-mediated myocarditis.
clinicalPearl: In laryngeal diphtheria, antitoxin neutralises circulating toxin and must be given immediately — never wait for culture results; treatment delay increases the risk of fatal myocarditis.
keyClue: Unvaccinated child with adherent greyish membrane that bleeds on removal, bull-neck, systemic toxicity, and stridor
commonExamTrap: Delaying antitoxin while waiting for culture confirmation of Corynebacterium diphtheriae — the diagnosis is clinical and antitoxin must be given urgently
missedConcept: Laryngeal diphtheria kills by two mechanisms: airway obstruction from the pseudomembrane AND exotoxin-mediated myocarditis and paralysis — both must be addressed
redFlagReason: Laryngeal diphtheria causes airway obstruction and fatal myocarditis; antitoxin must be given immediately without awaiting culture confirmation.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man presents with a 6-week history of unilateral left-sided conductive hearing loss and a sensation of fullness in the left ear. He has no history of recent upper respiratory infection. Otoscopy shows a dull, slightly retracted tympanic membrane on the left with evidence of fluid. Audiometry confirms conductive hearing loss of 30 dB with an air-bone gap. Decongestants tried for 4 weeks have not helped. What is the most important additional investigation to arrange?
options: Repeat audiometry in 3 months and prescribe intranasal corticosteroids
CT scan of the nasopharynx and sinuses to exclude infection or malignancy
Myringotomy and grommet insertion immediately without further investigation
Allergy testing and antihistamine therapy for 8 weeks
Pure tone audiometry only; the clinical picture is sufficient
explanation: Otitis media with effusion (OME) is common in children but uncommon in adults. When OME occurs in an adult, the nasopharynx must be carefully evaluated for malignancy, because nasopharyngeal carcinoma or other tumours can obstruct the Eustachian tube and cause unilateral middle ear effusion. CT scans of the nasopharynx and sinuses are indicated to exclude infection or tumour. In this patient with unilateral OME not responding to decongestants, nasopharyngeal pathology must be excluded before proceeding to myringotomy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Watchful waiting for another 3 months in an adult with unilateral OME risks missing nasopharyngeal carcinoma; the nasopharynx must be evaluated promptly.
This is correct — in adults, unilateral OME mandates CT scan of the nasopharynx to exclude malignancy; this is a key clinical distinction from paediatric OME.
Myringotomy may be appropriate later, but proceeding without first excluding nasopharyngeal tumour would miss a potentially curable cancer.
Allergy evaluation may be relevant with a suggestive history, but excluding nasopharyngeal malignancy takes precedence in an adult with unilateral OME.
Audiometry alone is insufficient; the key management priority is excluding nasopharyngeal tumour as the cause of the unilateral Eustachian tube obstruction.
clinicalPearl: Unilateral OME in an adult — unlike in children — requires urgent exclusion of nasopharyngeal carcinoma with CT scan before symptomatic treatment.
keyClue: Adult male, unilateral OME not resolving despite decongestants
commonExamTrap: Treating adult OME the same as paediatric OME with watchful waiting or grommet insertion without first excluding nasopharyngeal tumour
missedConcept: OME in adults has a different and more sinister differential than in children; nasopharyngeal carcinoma presents with unilateral conductive hearing loss from Eustachian tube obstruction

clinicalStem: A 67-year-old male heavy smoker and alcohol drinker presents with a 10-week history of progressive dysphagia, muffled voice, and left-sided neck mass. Flexible nasendoscopy reveals an exophytic mass involving the epiglottis and left aryepiglottic fold with normal bilateral vocal cord mobility. CT staging shows the tumour confined to the supraglottis without thyroid cartilage invasion and a single left-sided lymph node of 2.5 cm. Biopsy confirms squamous cell carcinoma. What is the correct T stage of this primary tumour?
options: T1 — limited to one subsite of supraglottis with normal cord mobility
T2 — invades mucosa of more than one adjacent subsite of supraglottis without laryngeal fixation
T3 — limited to larynx with vocal cord fixation
T4a — invades through thyroid cartilage
T4b — invades prevertebral space or encases carotid artery
explanation: The tumour involves more than one subsite of the supraglottis (epiglottis and aryepiglottic fold) but vocal cord mobility is normal and there is no laryngeal fixation or thyroid cartilage invasion. By the TNM staging system for supraglottic carcinoma, T2 is defined as a tumour that invades the mucosa of more than one adjacent subsite of the supraglottis or extends to the glottis or a region outside the supraglottis, without fixation of the larynx. The N stage is N1 (single ipsilateral node ≤ 3 cm).
wrongAnswerExplanations: T1 is limited to one subsite of the supraglottis with normal cord mobility; this tumour involves two subsites (epiglottis and aryepiglottic fold), so it exceeds T1.
This is correct — T2 supraglottic carcinoma involves more than one adjacent subsite without laryngeal fixation or thyroid cartilage invasion.
T3 requires vocal cord fixation or invasion of postcricoid area, pre-epiglottic tissues, or paraglottic space; the vocal cords are mobile here.
T4a requires invasion through thyroid cartilage or tissues beyond the larynx; the CT shows no cartilage invasion.
T4b requires prevertebral space invasion, carotid encasement, or mediastinal invasion — none of which are present.
clinicalPearl: In supraglottic carcinoma, normal vocal cord mobility is the key feature distinguishing T1/T2 from T3; always assess cord mobility as it directly determines staging and operability.
keyClue: Two supraglottic subsites involved (epiglottis and aryepiglottic fold) with normal vocal cord mobility and no cartilage invasion
commonExamTrap: Staging based on number of lymph nodes before assessing primary T stage; primary T stage depends on subsites involved and cord mobility, not nodal status
missedConcept: Vocal cord fixation is the watershed between T2 and T3 in both supraglottic and glottic carcinoma — cord mobility assessment is central to staging

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman presents with progressive bilateral hearing loss, worse on the right. She notices she hears better in noisy environments. Her father has a similar problem. Examination shows normal tympanic membranes. Rinne test is negative bilaterally (bone conduction > air conduction). Weber lateralises to the right. Audiometry shows bilateral conductive hearing loss with an air-bone gap, maximally 55 dB on the right, with normal bone conduction thresholds. What is the most appropriate surgical management for the right ear?
options: Myringotomy with grommet insertion to equalise middle ear pressure
Tympanoplasty to repair the suspected ossicular discontinuity
Stapedectomy for osteosclerotic fixation of the stapes footplate
Cochlear implant insertion as sensorineural loss is suspected
No intervention; prescribe hearing aid only as surgery carries no benefit
explanation: The clinical picture is classic otosclerosis: young woman, bilateral progressive conductive hearing loss, paracusis Willisii (better hearing in noise — Carhart notch physiology), positive family history, negative Rinne, and an audiogram showing conductive loss with air-bone gap and normal bone conduction. Otosclerosis involves osteosclerotic fixation of the stapes footplate. The definitive surgical treatment is stapedectomy, in which the fixed stapes is removed and replaced, restoring sound transmission to the oval window. A hearing aid is an alternative when surgery is refused or not possible.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Myringotomy is for middle ear effusion (OME); the tympanic membranes are normal and there is no fluid — this is otosclerosis, not OME.
Tympanoplasty repairs tympanic membrane perforations or the ossicular chain; this patient has stapes fixation, not disruption — the appropriate surgery is stapedectomy, not tympanoplasty.
This is correct — stapedectomy is the definitive surgical treatment for otosclerosis (osteosclerotic fixation of stapes footplate) and restores hearing by replacing the fixed stapes.
The bone conduction thresholds are normal, confirming this is purely conductive loss; cochlear implants are for severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss.
Surgery (stapedectomy) is highly effective for otosclerosis; a hearing aid is reserved for cases where surgery is contraindicated or refused, not as primary management in a young surgical candidate.
clinicalPearl: Bilateral progressive conductive hearing loss in a young adult with negative Rinne, normal tympanic membranes, and positive family history = otosclerosis; definitive treatment is stapedectomy.
keyClue: Negative Rinne, normal tympanic membranes, bilateral air-bone gap, positive family history, and paracusis (better hearing in noise)
commonExamTrap: Inserting grommets — which are for OME — into a patient with otosclerosis who has a normal-looking ear with no fluid
missedConcept: In otosclerosis, the stapes footplate becomes fixed and cannot vibrate; stapedectomy removes the fixed stapes and restores the ossicular chain

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents with sudden onset severe constant rotatory vertigo that began 36 hours ago and has not improved. She reports nausea and vomiting but no hearing loss, tinnitus, or ear fullness. She has no headache, diplopia, or limb weakness. On examination there is horizontal nystagmus that increases on gaze away from the side of the fast phase. The head impulse test is abnormal on the left. Pure tone audiometry is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Meniere's disease
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV)
Vestibular neuronitis
Acoustic neuroma
Central vertigo from posterior inferior cerebellar artery syndrome
explanation: Vestibular neuronitis presents with the hallmark of abrupt true-whirling vertigo lasting more than 24 hours, with no cochlear symptoms (no hearing loss or tinnitus) and no other neurological features. The abnormal head impulse test (positive catch-up saccade) confirms peripheral vestibular dysfunction on the affected side. This distinguishes it from BPPV (brief, positional, fatigable) and Meniere's disease (fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, aural fullness). The absence of headache, diplopia, or limb signs argues against central pathology.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Meniere's disease requires the tetrad of episodic vertigo, fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness; this patient has none of these cochlear symptoms.
BPPV causes brief episodes (under 1 minute) of vertigo triggered by specific head positions; this patient has constant vertigo lasting 36 hours — inconsistent with BPPV.
This is correct — abrupt constant vertigo over 24 hours, no cochlear symptoms, abnormal head impulse test, and no neurological features is the classic presentation of vestibular neuronitis.
Acoustic neuroma typically causes unsteadiness or vague motion sensation, not severe acute vertigo; it is also associated with sensorineural hearing loss on the affected side.
Central vertigo (e.g., PICA infarct) typically presents with additional neurological signs such as ipsilateral facial sensory loss, Horner syndrome, or limb ataxia — none of which are present here.
clinicalPearl: Vestibular neuronitis = abrupt prolonged vertigo (>24 h), no hearing loss, no tinnitus, positive head impulse test — differentiate from PICA stroke by absence of other neurological signs.
keyClue: Constant vertigo >24 hours, no cochlear symptoms, abnormal head impulse test, no neurological deficits
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing Meniere's disease when there is no hearing loss or tinnitus — Meniere's requires cochlear symptoms
missedConcept: The head impulse test (positive catch-up saccade toward the lesion) is the bedside test that confirms peripheral vestibular hypofunction in vestibular neuronitis

topic: Laryngeal cancer follow-up and radiation complications
clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man completed chemoradiotherapy for T2N0M0 glottic squamous cell carcinoma 18 months ago. He now presents with fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and dry skin. His voice is satisfactory and there is no evidence of tumour recurrence on flexible laryngoscopy. What complication of his prior treatment most likely explains his current symptoms?
options: Laryngeal radionecrosis causing chronic inflammation
Hypothyroidism as a complication of radiation to the neck
Recurrent laryngeal cancer presenting atypically
Xerostomia secondary to parotid gland irradiation
Stricture and fibrosis of the hypopharynx causing dysphagia
explanation: Fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and dry skin are classic features of hypothyroidism. Thyroid function is reduced after laryngeal cancer treatment that includes radiation therapy or extensive laryngeal and thyroid surgery. National Comprehensive Cancer Network guidelines recommend thyroid function tests every 6 to 12 months in patients with a history of neck radiation or open laryngeal surgery. Radiation to the neck affects thyroid vasculature and parenchyma, leading to delayed hypothyroidism that may manifest months to years after treatment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Radionecrosis would present with neck pain, fistula, exposed cartilage, or chondritis — not the systemic metabolic features described here.
This is correct — hypothyroidism is a well-documented late complication of radiation therapy to the neck for laryngeal cancer, manifesting with fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance.
Recurrent laryngeal cancer most commonly presents with worsening hoarseness, dysphagia, or neck mass; flexible laryngoscopy has already excluded this.
Xerostomia is dry mouth from parotid irradiation; it does not cause fatigue, weight gain, or cold intolerance.
Hypopharyngeal fibrosis causes progressive dysphagia; the metabolic features here point specifically to hypothyroidism, not a structural swallowing problem.
clinicalPearl: Thyroid function tests should be checked every 6–12 months after neck irradiation for laryngeal cancer — post-radiation hypothyroidism is a treatable late complication that must not be missed.
keyClue: History of radiation to the neck for laryngeal cancer + fatigue + weight gain + cold intolerance = hypothyroidism
commonExamTrap: Attributing the symptoms to tumour recurrence without checking thyroid function in a patient whose laryngoscopy is clear
missedConcept: Radiation to the neck damages the thyroid gland and its vasculature, causing hypothyroidism that may appear months to years after treatment — routine TFT monitoring is mandatory
